name: New Zealand
tagline: From the last great Polynesian voyages to a bicultural Pacific democracy founded on a contested treaty.
summary: New Zealand — Aotearoa — was among the last large landmasses on Earth to be settled, reached by Polynesian voyagers around 1300 CE whose descendants developed the distinct Māori world of iwi and hapū. European contact came with Abel Tasman's 1642 sighting and James Cook's accurate charting in 1769–70, followed by missionaries, traders, and the muskets that transformed inter-tribal warfare. On 6 February 1840 the British Crown and Māori chiefs signed the Treaty of Waitangi, the nation's founding document, whose English and Māori texts differ in meaning and whose interpretation has been contested ever since. Through the New Zealand Wars and land confiscation, the world-first grant of women's suffrage in 1893, Dominion status, the welfare state, and a post-1970s Māori renaissance, New Zealand became a prosperous bicultural democracy still working through the Treaty's unfinished promises.

founding.label: The Treaty of Waitangi
founding.detail: New Zealand's founding moment is the signing of the Treaty of Waitangi (Te Tiriti o Waitangi) on 6 February 1840 at Waitangi in the Bay of Islands, where the British Crown's representative Captain William Hobson and a group of Māori rangatira (chiefs) signed an agreement that sheets carried around the country would eventually bring more than 500 signatories. The Treaty established a basis for British authority in New Zealand while guaranteeing Māori the possession of their lands and properties. Crucially, its English and Māori texts are not exact translations of one another: where the English text has Māori cede 'sovereignty', the Māori text speaks of granting kāwanatanga (governorship) while retaining tino rangatiratanga (full chieftainship). That difference, and the meaning of the Treaty as a whole, has been contested from 1840 to the present and lies at the heart of New Zealand's constitutional life.

quickFacts.label: Māori settlement
quickFacts.value: Polynesian voyagers, around 1300 CE — among the last lands settled

quickFacts.label: World first
quickFacts.value: First self-governing nation to grant women the vote, 1893

eras.title: The Polynesian voyage and the making of the Māori world
eras.standfirst: Around 1300 CE, Polynesian voyagers in great ocean-going canoes reached Aotearoa, the last large temperate landmass to be settled, and over the following centuries developed the distinct Māori world of iwi, hapū, and migration traditions.
body: New Zealand was one of the last large landmasses on Earth to be settled by humans. The ancestors of the Māori reached Aotearoa from East Polynesia — the Society Islands, the southern Cook Islands, and the Austral Islands — probably in the late thirteenth century, around 1300 CE. They sailed in double-hulled voyaging canoes across thousands of kilometres of open ocean, navigating by the stars, winds, ocean swells, and the flight of birds. Rather than a single arrival, tradition records many canoes, and tribal groups trace their descent from the captains and crews of more than forty legendary vessels. The navigator Kupe is credited in some traditions with discovering the islands.

Over the following centuries the settlers adapted to a land far larger and cooler than the tropical islands of their origin. They became the Māori, organising society into iwi (tribes) and hapū (sub-tribes) bound by whakapapa (genealogy) and centred on the marae and the authority of rangatira (chiefs). They cultivated kūmara and other crops where the climate allowed, hunted the great flightless moa to extinction, fished, and gathered, and developed a rich material culture of carving, weaving, and the working of pounamu (greenstone). Mana, utu, and tapu structured a world of alliance, exchange, and warfare.

By the time Europeans arrived, Māori had spread across both main islands and out to the Chatham Islands, where settlers became the distinct Moriori people. The land itself was woven into identity: mountains, rivers, and harbours carried ancestral names and stories, and oral tradition preserved deep knowledge of genealogy, navigation, and history. This was a fully peopled, intricately known land — Aotearoa, 'the land of the long white cloud' — when the first European ship appeared off its coast in 1642.

events.title: Polynesian voyagers settle Aotearoa
events.summary: The ancestors of the Māori reached New Zealand from East Polynesia in great voyaging canoes around 1300 CE, making it one of the last large landmasses on Earth to be permanently settled.

events.title: The Māori world takes shape
events.summary: Settlers adapted to a temperate land and developed the distinct Māori culture of iwi and hapū, organised by whakapapa and rangatira and expressed through carving, weaving, and the working of pounamu.

events.title: Settlement of the Chatham Islands
events.summary: Māori reached the remote Chatham Islands (Rēkohu) over 750 km east of the mainland, where the settlers became the distinct Moriori people with their own traditions.

pullquote.text: New Zealand was one of the last large landmasses on Earth to be settled — Aotearoa, reached by ocean voyagers around 1300 CE.

eras.title: European contact, missionaries, and the musket wars
eras.standfirst: Abel Tasman's fleeting 1642 sighting and James Cook's accurate charting in 1769 opened Aotearoa to traders and missionaries, while the spread of muskets unleashed a generation of devastating inter-tribal warfare.
body: The first European to sight New Zealand was the Dutch navigator Abel Tasman, who reached the coast in December 1642. After a violent encounter with Māori in what he named Murderers' Bay (Golden Bay/Mohua), Tasman left without landing, but his pilot charted a fragment of the west coast that appeared in atlases for over two centuries. Sustained contact began with the British naval officer James Cook, who sighted the country on 6 October 1769 and, in just 175 days aboard HMB Endeavour, circumnavigated and charted both main islands with remarkable accuracy, mapping the coastline that the world would come to know.

From the 1790s sealers, whalers, and traders frequented the coasts, and in December 1814 the Anglican missionary Samuel Marsden conducted the first Christian service in New Zealand at Rangihoua in the Bay of Islands, under the protection of the Ngāpuhi chief Ruatara. Missionaries, flax and timber trading, and a growing Māori engagement with the wider world followed. European goods, crops, and literacy spread quickly, but so did new diseases and, above all, firearms — and the introduction of the musket would transform the balance of power among the iwi.

From the late 1810s into the 1830s, Māori chiefs who had acquired muskets waged campaigns of conquest and revenge against traditionally armed rivals in what became known as the musket wars. The Ngāti Toa leader Te Rauparaha used firearms and mobility to drive a great migration south from Kāwhia to the Kāpiti Coast and across Cook Strait into the northern South Island, devastating tribes such as Ngāi Tahu. Tens of thousands died and whole districts were depopulated or reshaped. As lawlessness and land transactions multiplied, Māori and the British Crown moved toward a formal agreement.

events.title: Abel Tasman sights New Zealand
events.summary: The Dutch navigator Abel Tasman became the first European to sight New Zealand, charting a fragment of the west coast but leaving without landing after a deadly clash with Māori in Golden Bay.

events.title: James Cook charts the islands
events.summary: James Cook sighted New Zealand and over 175 days circumnavigated and accurately charted both main islands aboard HMB Endeavour, fixing the country's outline for European maps.

events.title: First Christian mission
events.summary: Samuel Marsden conducted New Zealand's first Christian service at Rangihoua in the Bay of Islands, beginning the missionary presence that spread Christianity, literacy, and new ideas among Māori.

events.title: The musket wars
events.summary: The spread of firearms unleashed a generation of devastating inter-tribal warfare, with leaders such as Te Rauparaha driving migrations and conquests that killed tens of thousands and reshaped the tribal map.

figures.name: James Cook
figures.blurb: Charted both main islands of New Zealand with great accuracy during his first Pacific voyage in 1769–70, fixing the country's outline for European maps.

figures.name: Te Rauparaha
figures.blurb: A formidable war leader of the musket-wars era who led Ngāti Toa's migration south to Kāpiti and conquests across Cook Strait, earning the epithet 'the Napoleon of the South'.

pullquote.text: In just 175 days Cook sailed around the main islands, charting the coast with an accuracy that endured for generations.

eras.title: The Treaty of Waitangi, the New Zealand Wars, and confiscation
eras.standfirst: The Treaty of Waitangi of 1840 founded British New Zealand on an agreement whose two texts differed in meaning; within two decades, disputes over land and authority erupted into the New Zealand Wars and large-scale confiscation of Māori land.
body: On 6 February 1840, at Waitangi in the Bay of Islands, the Crown's representative William Hobson and a group of Māori rangatira signed the Treaty of Waitangi. Copies were then carried around the country, and by year's end more than 500 chiefs had signed. The Treaty is New Zealand's founding document, but its English and Māori texts are not exact translations. In the English text Māori cede 'sovereignty' to the Queen; in the Māori text — the one almost all signatories signed — they granted kāwanatanga (governorship) while keeping tino rangatiratanga, their full chiefly authority over lands and treasures. The gulf between these meanings has been debated ever since and remains central to New Zealand's politics.

As organised British settlement expanded under the New Zealand Company and the Crown, settler demand for land collided with Māori reluctance to sell and with the Treaty's guarantees. Conflict first flared in the north in the mid-1840s, then escalated dramatically from 1860 when fighting broke out in Taranaki and spread to the Waikato. The Kīngitanga, the Māori King movement formed in the 1850s to unite tribes and hold the land, became a focus of resistance. The wars pitted imperial troops and colonial forces, with some Māori allies, against iwi defending their territory and autonomy in a series of campaigns across the North Island.

To punish 'rebellion' and to free land for settlers, the colonial government passed the New Zealand Settlements Act 1863, under which the Crown confiscated — raupatu — vast tracts of Māori land, especially in the Waikato and Taranaki. About 1.2 million hectares were taken, often including the land of Māori who had not fought against the Crown. The wars and confiscation broke the economic and political power of many iwi, entrenched grievances that would last for generations, and left a legacy that the Treaty settlement process would only begin to address more than a century later.

events.title: Treaty of Waitangi signed
events.summary: The Crown and Māori rangatira signed the Treaty of Waitangi, founding British New Zealand; its differing English and Māori texts — over sovereignty versus governorship — left a meaning contested to this day.

events.title: The New Zealand Wars
events.summary: Disputes over land and sovereignty erupted into war in Taranaki in 1860 and spread across the North Island, pitting imperial and colonial forces against iwi defending their land and autonomy until about 1872.

events.title: Land confiscation legalised (raupatu)
events.summary: The New Zealand Settlements Act 1863 gave a legal cloak to the confiscation of about 1.2 million hectares of Māori land, chiefly in the Waikato and Taranaki, often taking land from iwi who had not fought the Crown.

figures.name: William Hobson
figures.blurb: As the Crown's representative he drafted and secured the Treaty of Waitangi in 1840 and became New Zealand's first governor, establishing British authority over the new colony.

pullquote.text: In the English text Māori ceded sovereignty; in the Māori text they granted governorship while keeping their chieftainship — a difference debated ever since.
pullquote.attribution: On the two texts of the Treaty of Waitangi

eras.title: Colony, women's suffrage, and Dominion
eras.standfirst: As the settler colony prospered and built parliamentary self-government, New Zealand made history in 1893 as the first self-governing nation to grant women the vote, and in 1907 it became a Dominion within the British Empire.
body: With the wars over, the settler colony grew rapidly on the back of wool, gold, and — after refrigerated shipping began in 1882 — frozen meat and dairy exports to Britain. Granted representative government from the 1850s, New Zealand developed a vigorous parliamentary politics and, in the early 1890s, a reforming Liberal government that pioneered measures in labour, land, and welfare that won the country an international reputation as a 'social laboratory'. Settler society increasingly saw itself as a distinct nation, even as it remained closely tied — economically, culturally, and constitutionally — to Britain.

The colony's most celebrated reform came on 19 September 1893, when New Zealand became the first self-governing country in the world to grant women the right to vote in parliamentary elections. The victory crowned a campaign led by Kate Sheppard and the Women's Christian Temperance Union, whose 1893 petition gathered nearly 32,000 signatures — close to a quarter of the adult European women in the country. At the general election ten weeks later, women turned out in large numbers, and New Zealand's example resonated through suffrage movements abroad.

Māori, meanwhile, navigated a colony built on their dispossession. Four Māori parliamentary seats had been created in 1867, giving Māori men the vote, and leaders worked within and outside the system to defend land and revive their communities. In 1907 New Zealand's constitutional status was raised from colony to Dominion, a symbolic step toward nationhood that changed little in practice but reflected a growing, if still loyal, sense of national identity within the British Empire on the eve of the First World War.

events.title: First refrigerated meat shipment
events.summary: The first cargo of frozen meat sailed from New Zealand to Britain, launching the refrigerated export trade in meat and dairy that would underpin the country's economy for a century.

events.title: Women win the vote
events.summary: New Zealand became the first self-governing nation in the world to grant women the parliamentary vote, the culmination of a petition campaign led by Kate Sheppard and the temperance movement.

events.title: New Zealand becomes a Dominion
events.summary: New Zealand's status was raised from colony to Dominion within the British Empire, a symbolic advance toward nationhood that left its close ties to Britain largely intact.

figures.name: Kate Sheppard
figures.blurb: Led the campaign that made New Zealand the first self-governing nation to grant women the vote in 1893, and became founding president of the National Council of Women.

pullquote.text: In 1893 New Zealand became the first self-governing nation in the world to grant the vote to all women.
pullquote.attribution: NZ History, Ministry for Culture and Heritage

eras.title: Nationhood, the world wars, and the welfare state
eras.standfirst: Two world wars deepened New Zealand's sense of nationhood even as it moved toward full legal independence, while the Labour government of 1935 built one of the world's most comprehensive welfare states.
body: New Zealand entered the First World War alongside Britain in 1914, and the landing of the Australian and New Zealand Army Corps (ANZAC) at Gallipoli on 25 April 1915 became a defining national experience, commemorated each year on Anzac Day. The war's terrible losses — and those of the Second World War, in which New Zealand again fought from 1939 — forged a stronger national identity while reinforcing ties of loyalty and sentiment to Britain. Māori served in both wars, including the celebrated Māori Battalion, even as their communities continued to press for justice over land and the Treaty.

In these decades Māori leadership found new voices. Apirana Ngata, the first Māori to complete a New Zealand university degree and member of Parliament for Eastern Māori from 1905 to 1943, worked to revive Māori land development, arts, and culture while operating within the colonial political system. Yet Māori remained economically marginalised, and the gap between the Treaty's promises and the realities of confiscation and assimilation pressure persisted, setting the stage for later demands for redress.

The Great Depression hit New Zealand hard, and in 1935 voters elected the first Labour government, which responded by building a welfare state. The Social Security Act 1938 created a comprehensive system of pensions and benefits and laid the foundations of a publicly funded health service — a 'cradle to grave' settlement widely admired abroad. Constitutionally, the country edged toward independence: having become a Dominion in 1907, New Zealand finally adopted the Statute of Westminster in 1947, removing the British Parliament's power to legislate for it and confirming full legal independence.

events.title: Gallipoli and the Anzac legend
events.summary: New Zealand and Australian troops landed at Gallipoli, a costly campaign that, despite ending in withdrawal, became a foundational national story commemorated each year on Anzac Day.

events.title: The welfare state
events.summary: The first Labour government's Social Security Act 1938 created a comprehensive system of pensions, benefits, and public health, building a 'cradle to grave' welfare state admired around the world.

events.title: Statute of Westminster adopted
events.summary: New Zealand adopted the Statute of Westminster, ending the British Parliament's power to legislate for it and confirming the country's full legal independence — the last Dominion to do so.

figures.name: Apirana Ngata
figures.role: Māori leader and Member of Parliament
figures.blurb: The first Māori to complete a New Zealand university degree; as MP for Eastern Māori (1905–43) he championed Māori land development and a renaissance of Māori arts and culture.

pullquote.text: New Zealand was the last of the Dominions to adopt the Statute of Westminster, confirming its full legal independence in 1947.

eras.title: The Māori renaissance and bicultural New Zealand
eras.standfirst: From the 1970s a Māori cultural and legal renaissance reshaped the nation: the Waitangi Tribunal, Treaty settlements, and the revival of te reo Māori turned New Zealand toward an explicitly bicultural identity by the present day.
body: A wave of Māori protest and cultural revival from the late 1960s transformed New Zealand's understanding of itself. Activists pressed for land rights, the survival of te reo Māori, and honouring of the Treaty, culminating in the 1975 land march and growing public attention to long-ignored grievances. In response, Parliament passed the Treaty of Waitangi Act 1975, creating the Waitangi Tribunal — a standing commission to investigate Māori claims that the Crown had breached the Treaty's principles. In 1985 its jurisdiction was extended back to 1840, opening the way to address historic confiscations and dispossession.

The Tribunal's findings reframed New Zealand law and politics around the 'principles of the Treaty', and from the 1990s the Crown negotiated major settlements with iwi. Waikato-Tainui (1995) and Ngāi Tahu (1998) received formal Crown apologies and redress for the wars and confiscations of the nineteenth century, models followed by many later settlements. Alongside this, the Māori Language Act 1987 recognised te reo Māori as an official language and established a commission to promote it, anchoring a wider revival of language, education through kōhanga reo and kura kaupapa, and Māori broadcasting.

Modern New Zealand is a prosperous, outward-looking Pacific democracy that has progressively defined itself as bicultural, with the Treaty of Waitangi as a living constitutional touchstone rather than a closed historical document. Tongariro National Park (a dual natural and cultural World Heritage site) and Te Wahipounamu in the south are inscribed by UNESCO, recognising both natural grandeur and Māori spiritual significance. Debates over the Treaty's meaning, the pace and fairness of settlements, and the balance between Māori and Crown authority remain vigorous — a sign that the questions opened at Waitangi in 1840 are still being worked out in 2026.

events.title: Waitangi Tribunal established
events.summary: The Treaty of Waitangi Act 1975 created the Waitangi Tribunal to investigate Crown breaches of the Treaty's principles; in 1985 its mandate was extended back to 1840, enabling redress for historic wrongs.

events.title: Te reo Māori made official
events.summary: The Māori Language Act 1987 recognised te reo Māori as an official language and created a commission to promote it, a landmark in the post-1970s Māori cultural renaissance.

events.title: Waikato-Tainui settlement and Crown apology
events.summary: The Crown signed a Deed of Settlement with Waikato-Tainui including a formal royal apology for the wars and confiscations of the 1860s, a model for later Treaty settlements such as Ngāi Tahu in 1998.

events.title: UNESCO World Heritage recognition
events.summary: Tongariro National Park — inscribed for its dual natural and Māori cultural significance — and Te Wahipounamu in the south were recognised by UNESCO, honouring both landscape and Māori heritage.

pullquote.text: The questions opened at Waitangi in 1840 — sovereignty, governorship, and chieftainship — are still being worked out today.

figures.name: Te Rauparaha
figures.blurb: A formidable war leader of the musket-wars era who led Ngāti Toa's migration south and conquests across Cook Strait, and later signed the Treaty of Waitangi.

figures.name: Kate Sheppard
figures.blurb: Led the campaign that made New Zealand the first self-governing nation to grant women the vote in 1893.

figures.name: Apirana Ngata
figures.role: Māori leader and Member of Parliament
figures.blurb: First Māori university graduate and long-serving MP who championed Māori land development and a renaissance of Māori arts and culture.
